channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: EYE NEWSFLASH: Wes Streeting resignation adds fresh instability for housing market
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market faces renewed uncertainty following the resignation of Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, from Sir Keir Starmer’s Cabinet. In a sharply worded letter published online, Streeting expressed his loss of confidence in the Prime Minister’s leadership, describing a lack of vision and direction within the government. This development comes at a challenging time for Labour, with the party grappling with poor local election results and growing internal tensions.

Political Instability and Its Impact on the Housing Market

Streeting’s resignation has raised fresh concerns about political stability and its potential effects on the property sector. The housing market is already navigating a complex landscape, characterised by affordability pressures, higher mortgage rates, and subdued transaction levels. Any prolonged uncertainty in leadership could further dampen investor confidence and delay key housing reforms currently progressing through Parliament.

The property industry relies heavily on stable economic and political conditions to foster growth and encourage investment. With Labour’s internal unrest now under the spotlight, homeowners and prospective buyers may feel cautious about making significant financial decisions. This sentiment could lead to a slowdown in market activity, exacerbating existing challenges in the housing sector.

Housing Policy and Economic Direction

The resignation also highlights broader questions about the government’s economic direction and its commitment to housing reforms. Policies aimed at addressing the UK’s housing crisis, such as increasing affordable housing supply and improving rental market conditions, may face delays or revisions amidst political turbulence. This could leave many borrowers and homeowners in limbo, unsure of how upcoming changes might affect their finances.

For those considering entering the property market, it could be worth monitoring developments closely and seeking professional advice to navigate potential uncertainties. Similarly, homeowners may wish to review their current mortgage arrangements to ensure they are prepared for any shifts in housing policy or economic conditions.

Whilst the full implications of Streeting’s resignation remain to be seen, it serves as a reminder of the interconnectedness between politics and the housing market. Borrowers and investors alike may need to remain vigilant as the situation unfolds.

As the UK property sector continues to face headwinds, staying informed and proactive could help mitigate risks associated with political and economic instability. Whether you’re a first-time buyer, remortgaging, or investing in property, understanding the broader context can provide valuable insights for making informed decisions.

item.title: Contractor appointed to deliver Connells Group’s new Milton Keynes HQ
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Connells Group has taken a significant step in consolidating its operations with the appointment of Woodhouse as contractor for its new Milton Keynes headquarters. The estate agency giant, owned by Skipton Building Society, will relocate from its current Leighton Buzzard base to the newly fitted-out Witan Gate House – a 60,000 sq ft office development designed to accommodate over 500 employees.

Modern workspace design

The Milton Keynes headquarters will feature contemporary workspace solutions tailored to modern working practices. The layout includes:

  • Open-plan workstations to encourage collaboration
  • Designated quiet zones for focused tasks
  • Flexible meeting and social spaces
  • A multi-purpose training suite for company-wide events
  • Executive office accommodation

This consolidation project will unite teams currently spread across multiple locations, potentially improving operational efficiency and company culture. Woodhouse will oversee the entire fit-out process from initial works through to completion.

Strategic expansion continues

The headquarters development coincides with Connells Group's ongoing expansion strategy. The company recently acquired Crofts Estate Agents, adding three Lincolnshire branches to its network. With over 1,200 branches nationwide and approximately 16,000 employees across estate agency, lettings and mortgage services, the group continues to strengthen its position in the UK property market.

Businesses considering relocation or expansion might note how such headquarters projects can bring operational benefits. The Milton Keynes location offers strong transport links and access to a skilled workforce, factors that often influence corporate relocation decisions.

As the property sector evolves, major players like Connells Group are adapting their physical footprints to meet changing workplace expectations. This investment in modern, purpose-designed premises reflects broader trends in commercial property utilisation post-pandemic.

item.title: New hire as Ellisons strengthens residential property team
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Ellisons Solicitors has recently expanded its residential property team with the appointment of Sarah Osborne, a seasoned legal professional, as a senior associate. Based in Bury St Edmunds, Osborne brings a wealth of expertise in leasehold enfranchisement, bolstering the firm’s service offerings in this specialised area of property law.

Expertise in Leasehold Enfranchisement

Leasehold enfranchisement, the process by which leaseholders can extend their lease or purchase the freehold of their property, is a complex and often challenging area of law. With Osborne’s appointment, Ellisons aims to enhance its ability to guide clients through these intricate procedures. Her experience is expected to provide valuable support to homeowners navigating the legal landscape of leasehold properties.

This move comes at a time when leasehold reform remains a hot topic in the UK property market. Homeowners may find it beneficial to seek expert advice when considering their options, particularly as legislative changes continue to shape the sector.

A Strategic Addition to the Team

Osborne’s arrival underscores Ellisons’ commitment to strengthening its residential property services. Her role will focus on assisting clients with leasehold matters, including lease extensions, collective enfranchisement, and right-to-manage claims. This addition to the team reflects the firm’s dedication to providing comprehensive legal support to property owners.

For those dealing with leasehold issues, it could be worth consulting specialists like Ellisons to ensure a smooth and legally sound process. Osborne’s expertise is set to play a key role in delivering tailored solutions to clients across Suffolk and beyond.

As the property market evolves, Ellisons’ investment in its residential team highlights the importance of specialised legal knowledge in addressing the needs of homeowners. Borrowers and property investors alike might consider the value of professional guidance when tackling leasehold complexities.

item.title: The Mortgage Lender enhances BTL range with rate cuts
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Buy-to-Let Mortgage Rates See Competitive Reductions

The Mortgage Lender has announced significant rate reductions across its buy-to-let mortgage range, alongside the reintroduction of products at 75% loan-to-value (LTV). This development may interest landlords and property investors seeking more competitive financing options in the current market.

What's Changed for Buy-to-Let Borrowers?

The lender has implemented cuts across its BTL product range, making its mortgage offerings potentially more attractive to property investors. Key changes include:

  • Rate reductions across various fixed-rate products
  • Reintroduction of mortgages at 75% LTV
  • Options available for both individual and limited company borrowers

Why This Matters for Property Investors

With buy-to-let investors facing multiple challenges including higher interest rates and regulatory changes, these rate reductions could provide some welcome relief. The return of 75% LTV products might particularly appeal to landlords looking to:

  • Refinance existing properties
  • Expand their portfolios with lower deposit requirements
  • Access more competitive rates than currently available elsewhere

As always with mortgage products, borrowers might consider comparing rates across multiple lenders and consulting with a qualified mortgage adviser to ensure they find the most suitable option for their circumstances. The buy-to-let market continues to evolve, and staying informed about product changes could help investors make timely financial decisions.

item.title: EYE NEWS UPDATE: Property industry reacts to the King’s Speech
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The government has placed housing reform at the forefront of its agenda, as outlined in the recent King’s Speech. Delivered in the House of Lords, the speech unveiled 37 new bills for the upcoming parliamentary session, with several measures set to impact the property sector significantly. Among the key proposals are reforms to leasehold arrangements, social housing protections, and building safety regulations.

Leasehold Reform Takes Centre Stage

One of the most notable announcements was the introduction of the Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill. This legislation aims to ban the sale of new leasehold flats in England and Wales, while also capping ground rents at £250 per year. These changes are part of a broader effort to overhaul the leasehold system, which has long been criticised for its complexity and high costs for homeowners.

The reforms are expected to have far-reaching implications for developers, freeholders, managing agents, and lenders. By simplifying property ownership structures and reducing ongoing expenses for leaseholders, the government hopes to create a fairer system. Borrowers and homeowners may wish to monitor these developments closely, as they could influence future property transactions and ownership arrangements.

Social Housing Protections Strengthened

Another key proposal is the Social Housing Renewal Bill, designed to enhance protections for social tenants and preserve affordable housing stock. Under the new measures, newly built social homes in England would be exempt from the Right to Buy scheme for 35 years. This move aims to address concerns about the rapid loss of affordable housing and ensure long-term availability for those in need.

Additionally, the bill introduces safeguards for victims of domestic abuse living in social housing, allowing them to remain in their homes in certain circumstances. These changes reflect a growing focus on tenant welfare and could provide greater stability for vulnerable individuals and families.

Building Safety Measures Expanded

Building safety remains a critical priority, with the introduction of the Remediation Bill. This legislation grants ministers new powers to require construction product manufacturers to contribute to the cost of removing unsafe cladding from residential buildings. It forms part of the government’s ongoing response to the building safety crisis, which has seen pressure mount on developers, manufacturers, and freeholders to address remediation costs and delays.

The measures aim to accelerate the removal of hazardous materials and improve safety standards across the housing sector. Property professionals and homeowners alike may wish to stay informed about these developments, as they could impact building maintenance costs and safety compliance requirements.

The King’s Speech signals a busy legislative period ahead for the housing sector, with agents, landlords, developers, and housing associations awaiting further details on implementation. These reforms could bring significant changes to property ownership, tenant protections, and building safety, shaping the landscape of the UK housing market for years to come.

item.title: Ex-housing secretary Angela Rayner cleared by HMRC over tax affairs
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Angela Rayner, the former housing secretary and deputy prime minister, has been cleared by HMRC over her tax affairs following an investigation. The inquiry centred on the purchase of an £800,000 flat in Hove, where Rayner initially paid a lower stamp duty rate. Whilst she has since repaid approximately £40,000 in underpaid tax, HMRC reportedly found no evidence of deliberate wrongdoing or negligence. This outcome removes a potential political distraction amidst ongoing scrutiny of Labour’s leadership following the party’s recent local election setbacks.

Background to the Investigation

The controversy began when questions arose over the stamp duty paid on Rayner’s property purchase. The former housing secretary acknowledged the underpayment and voluntarily repaid the outstanding amount last September, stepping down from her ministerial roles shortly afterwards. HMRC’s decision not to impose any financial penalties suggests that the underpayment was not deemed intentional. In a statement, Rayner emphasised her commitment to integrity and cooperation with the investigation, stating that politicians should be held to high standards.

Rayner’s political career has remained under the spotlight, particularly as Labour faces growing pressure to rebuild its electoral appeal. Although she has not ruled out a future leadership bid, Rayner has clarified that she would not actively seek to challenge current leader Sir Keir Starmer. Her focus appears to be on maintaining her role within the party whilst addressing broader policy issues.

Impact on the Housing Sector

As housing secretary, Rayner played a pivotal role in shaping legislation affecting the private rented sector, including the Renters’ Rights Act and the Planning and Infrastructure Act. Her tenure also saw ambitious targets set to deliver 1.5 million new homes in England during the current parliamentary term. These reforms continue to influence the housing market, with regulatory changes remaining a key concern for property professionals.

Rayner is scheduled to speak at the Propertymark One conference on 12 June at ExCeL London, an event that has sparked debate within the industry. Propertymark CEO Nathan Emerson acknowledged the decision to invite Rayner as a speaker was “without doubt a controversial choice”, particularly given the recent scrutiny of her tax affairs. Despite criticism from some agents, Emerson defended the decision, emphasising the importance of diverse perspectives in shaping the future of the housing sector.

The conference will provide a platform for Rayner to share her insights on ongoing housing reforms and the challenges facing the industry. Her address is expected to draw significant attention, reflecting her continued influence on housing policy and the broader political landscape.

As the housing sector navigates ongoing legislative and regulatory changes, Rayner’s involvement in key industry events highlights the intersection of politics and property. Whilst her tax affairs may have dominated headlines, her contributions to housing policy remain a focal point for professionals and policymakers alike.

item.title: JLL downgrades house price forecast but increases rental
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property market analysts at JLL have revised their UK housing forecasts, predicting slower house price growth but stronger rental increases by 2026. The updated outlook reflects ongoing economic pressures and shifting dynamics within the property sector.

House Price Growth Expectations Lowered

JLL has adjusted its house price forecast, anticipating a more modest rise in property values over the next few years. The firm cites factors such as higher mortgage rates, affordability challenges, and economic uncertainty as key contributors to this downgrade. Whilst house prices are still expected to increase, the pace of growth is now projected to be slower than previously estimated.

Homeowners and potential buyers might wish to consider how these projections could impact their long-term property plans. For those looking to purchase, the slower growth could present opportunities to enter the market at a more stable pace. However, it’s important to weigh this against the cost of borrowing, which remains elevated compared to recent years.

Rental Market Set for Strong Growth

In contrast to the housing market, JLL has upgraded its rental growth forecast, predicting a more robust increase in rental prices by 2026. This shift is attributed to sustained demand for rental properties, coupled with limited supply in many areas. Factors such as higher mortgage costs and changing tenant demographics are also driving this trend.

For landlords, this could mean stronger yields and increased rental income potential. However, tenants may face higher costs, particularly in high-demand areas. Those renting might consider reviewing their budgets and exploring longer-term rental agreements to mitigate potential price hikes.

Market Implications for Borrowers and Investors

The diverging forecasts for house prices and rental growth highlight the complexities of the current property market. Borrowers might find it beneficial to assess their financial position carefully, particularly when deciding between buying and renting. Investors, on the other hand, could focus on the rental market’s potential for stronger returns.

It’s worth noting that market conditions can change rapidly, and forecasts are subject to revision based on economic developments. Staying informed and seeking professional advice could help individuals navigate these uncertainties effectively.

As the UK property market continues to evolve, JLL’s updated forecasts offer valuable insights for homeowners, renters, and investors alike. Keeping a close eye on market trends and considering long-term goals could prove essential in making informed property decisions.

item.title: RICS releases updated home valuation guidance for flats in buildings with cladding
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New RICS Valuation Guidance for Flats in Buildings With Cladding

The Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS) has released updated valuation standards for flats in buildings with external cladding systems. The revised guidance, which comes into effect in November 2026, aims to create a more consistent approach to assessing lending risks while reducing unnecessary paperwork for homeowners and buyers.

This second edition of the professional standard focuses on when surveyors should request an EWS1 form during mortgage valuations. The framework establishes clear criteria based on building height and construction features, including cladding materials, curtain wall systems and balcony designs. Buildings are categorised into three groups: those over six storeys, those between five and six storeys, and those of four storeys or fewer.

Streamlining the Valuation Process

The updated guidance seeks to address industry concerns about excessive EWS1 requests, which have caused delays in some property transactions. Under the new standard, valuers should only request the form when there are specific reasons to assess potential risks to a property's value or marketability.

Key aspects of the revised framework include:

  • Clearer definitions of when cladding-related documentation is required
  • Recognition of alternative fire safety assessments in certain cases
  • Confirmation that EWS1 forms are solely for valuation purposes
  • Differentiation between various building types and heights

RICS emphasises that the EWS1 form should not be confused with a fire safety certificate, nor does it replace a formal fire risk assessment. Homebuyers and their legal advisers might wish to review a building's fire risk assessment separately as part of the conveyancing process.

Supporting Informed Lending Decisions

The standard also references PAS 9980 fire risk appraisals, acknowledging that in some circumstances, these reports could provide sufficient information for valuation purposes without requiring a full EWS1 form. This applies particularly when the appraisal clearly states whether remedial works are needed and has been completed by a qualified professional.

Nigel Sellars, RICS senior specialist for residential valuation, commented: "Mortgage valuations must provide lenders and borrowers with clear, proportionate advice. These updated criteria help identify when cladding information is genuinely needed while preventing unnecessary hurdles in the homebuying process."

The guidance follows extensive consultation with fire safety experts, valuers, insurers and mortgage lenders. Its implementation is expected to bring greater consistency to valuation practices while maintaining appropriate safeguards for properties where external wall systems may affect value.

The standard also references PAS 9980 fire risk appraisals, acknowledging that in some circumstances, these reports could provide sufficient information for valuation purposes without requiring a full EWS1 form. This applies particularly when the appraisal clearly states whether remedial works are needed and has been completed by a qualified professional.

Nigel Sellars, RICS senior specialist for residential valuation, commented: "Mortgage valuations must provide lenders and borrowers with clear, proportionate advice. These updated criteria help identify when cladding information is genuinely needed while preventing unnecessary hurdles in the homebuying process."

The guidance follows extensive consultation with fire safety experts, valuers, insurers and mortgage lenders. Its implementation is expected to bring greater consistency to valuation practices while maintaining appropriate safeguards for properties where external wall systems may affect value.

item.title: AI property search goes mainstream
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Artificial intelligence is reshaping how prospective buyers search for properties, with new tools offering personalised recommendations that challenge traditional portal browsing. Industry analysts suggest these AI-driven platforms could significantly alter home-hunting habits in the coming years.

How AI is changing property searches

Modern property search tools now use machine learning to analyse users' preferences, browsing history and budget constraints. Unlike conventional portals that require manual filtering, these systems automatically surface relevant listings based on sophisticated algorithms. Some platforms even factor in commute times, local amenities and future development plans when suggesting properties.

Key features of AI property search include:

  • Personalised recommendations that improve with use
  • Predictive pricing based on market trends
  • Virtual neighbourhood tours using augmented reality
  • Automated alerts for properties matching exact criteria

The impact on traditional property portals

While established property websites still dominate the market, industry observers note growing interest in AI alternatives. These intelligent systems potentially reduce the time buyers spend scrolling through irrelevant listings, though some experts caution they may create filter bubbles that limit exposure to diverse options.

For homeowners considering selling, these developments highlight the importance of:

  • Accurate property descriptions with comprehensive details
  • High-quality photographs and virtual tours
  • Competitive pricing based on local market conditions

As technology evolves, both buyers and sellers may benefit from exploring these emerging tools alongside traditional methods to ensure they don't miss opportunities in an increasingly digital property market.

item.title: Flexibility around booking can boost holiday let revenue, expert says
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Holiday let owners looking to maximise their rental income during peak seasons might consider offering more flexible booking arrangements. Industry experts suggest that adaptable cancellation policies and shorter minimum stays could help properties stand out in competitive markets, particularly around bank holiday periods when demand fluctuates.

The benefits of flexible booking terms

Research indicates that travellers increasingly prioritise flexibility when choosing holiday accommodation. Property owners who accommodate this preference may see several advantages:

  • Higher occupancy rates during traditionally slower periods
  • Increased appeal to last-minute bookers
  • Potential to command premium rates for short-notice availability
  • Improved guest satisfaction and repeat bookings

Whilst fixed weekly lets remain popular in some locations, many successful holiday let businesses now combine traditional bookings with more dynamic options. This hybrid approach allows owners to maintain steady income whilst capitalising on spontaneous travel trends.

Implementing flexibility without compromising income

Introducing adaptable booking policies requires careful planning to avoid revenue loss. Holiday let operators might consider:

  • Setting tiered cancellation policies (full refund up to 14 days, partial refund thereafter)
  • Offering 2-3 night minimum stays during peak weekends
  • Using dynamic pricing tools to adjust rates based on demand
  • Creating package deals for mid-week vacancies

Technology plays a crucial role in managing flexible bookings efficiently. Many property management systems now automate availability updates across multiple platforms, helping owners fill gaps in their calendars whilst maintaining control over pricing and occupancy.

As the holiday let market continues to evolve, flexibility appears to be becoming a key differentiator for successful properties. By carefully balancing guest convenience with business needs, owners may find they can boost both occupancy and revenue throughout the year.

item.title: Housing market loses momentum as buyers hold back, says RICS
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market continued to face challenges in April, with buyer activity remaining subdued amidst ongoing economic uncertainty and higher mortgage costs, according to the latest Residential Market Survey from the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS). The data suggests that whilst the pace of decline in buyer demand has eased slightly, the market remains under pressure, particularly in certain regions.

Buyer Demand and Sales Outlook

Buyer enquiries remained in negative territory in April, with a net balance of -34%, though this marked a slight improvement from March’s -40%. Agreed sales also stayed weak, recording a net balance of -36%. Surveyors expressed a cautious outlook for the months ahead, with short-term sales expectations remaining negative at -32%. The twelve-month outlook dipped just below neutral, indicating that many agents anticipate market conditions to remain flat for much of the year.

This subdued activity reflects broader economic headwinds, including concerns over inflation and the potential for further interest rate rises. With borrowing costs still elevated, many prospective buyers appear to be holding back, waiting for greater clarity on the economic landscape before committing to a purchase.

Supply and Regional Price Trends

Supply levels showed little movement in April, but there are indications that future stock levels could tighten further. Whilst new instructions remained broadly stable, the measure tracking new appraisals fell back into negative territory, suggesting fewer homeowners may be preparing to list their properties.

House prices also faced increased pressure during the month, with the headline RICS house price balance dropping to -34% from -25% in March. Regional variations were notable, with the sharpest price declines reported in London, the South East, East Anglia, and the South West. In contrast, areas such as the North West, Northern England, Scotland, and Northern Ireland demonstrated more resilient pricing trends.

Long-Term Expectations and Rental Market

Despite the near-term challenges, long-term expectations for the housing market remain relatively stable. Whilst short-term price expectations stayed negative, the twelve-month outlook remained marginally positive, albeit at its weakest level since late 2023.

The lettings market continues to grapple with a supply-demand imbalance. Tenant demand increased in April, whilst landlord instructions remained in decline, exacerbating pressure on rental stock levels. A net balance of respondents anticipates that rents will continue to rise in the coming months, reflecting the ongoing challenges faced by tenants in a competitive market.

Tarrant Parsons, Head of Market Research and Analysis at RICS, commented: “April’s results highlight a housing market still navigating significant macro headwinds, including geopolitical uncertainties and inflationary pressures. Until there is greater clarity on inflation and borrowing costs, market activity and sentiment are likely to remain subdued, particularly in southern England.”

Overall, the latest RICS survey paints a picture of a housing market in flux, with regional disparities and economic uncertainties shaping buyer and seller behaviour. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor developments closely as the year progresses.

item.title: New landlord tax hike set to filter through to renters
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Renters across the UK may see their housing costs climb from 2027 as landlords respond to upcoming tax changes, according to recent industry research. The Autumn Budget 2023 confirmed a two percentage point increase in income tax rates on property income, set to take effect in April 2027. With many property owners considering how to absorb these additional costs, tenants could bear the brunt through higher rents or reduced rental supply.

Landlords consider rent rises and property sales

A survey conducted for the National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) reveals nearly half of landlords (46%) plan to increase rents within the next year in response to the tax changes. The research indicates that 35% expect to implement larger rent hikes than previously anticipated, while a third (33%) are contemplating selling some or all of their rental properties. These findings align with earlier projections from the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), which warned that the policy could contribute to rising rental prices across the private sector.

Housing minister Matthew Pennycook has acknowledged that successive tax changes under different governments have influenced landlords' decisions to exit the market. This comes amid ongoing industry concerns about how taxation policies affect both rental supply and landlords' business strategies.

Growing pressures on renters

The potential for higher rents coincides with frozen housing benefit levels, creating particular challenges for tenants who rely on financial support. With private rents continuing to rise in many regions, affordability pressures are mounting for households across the rental market. The Institute for Fiscal Studies has highlighted inconsistencies in the current system, noting how variations in local housing support can create uncertainty for renters and disparities between different areas.

Recent data shows rental arrears reaching record levels, underscoring the financial strain facing many tenants. As landlords adjust their strategies in response to the changing tax landscape, renters may wish to review their budgeting and explore available support options to prepare for potential cost increases in the coming years.

With the 2027 tax changes still some way off, both landlords and tenants have time to consider their options. However, the research suggests the private rental market could see significant shifts as the implementation date approaches, potentially reshaping affordability and availability for UK renters.

item.title: Voice of the Agent returns with salaries, prime property and a new industry ‘faculty’
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Voice of the Agent, a prominent initiative in the UK property sector, has expanded its reach with the release of two new reports and the introduction of a training platform tailored for estate agency professionals. The latest publications, titled "The Voice of the Agent Part Four: People & Salary" and "The Voice of the Agent: The Prime Cut," delve into critical areas such as recruitment, salaries, leadership, and the prime property market. Additionally, the newly launched initiative, The Faculty, aims to transform industry insights into practical business training, marking a significant evolution for the platform.

People & Salary: Insights into the Estate Agency Workforce

The fourth instalment of The Voice of the Agent focuses on the individuals driving the estate and lettings agency sectors. The report examines salary structures, leadership dynamics, professional pressures, and career progression, shedding light on the challenges and opportunities within the industry. Key findings reveal that leadership roles remain predominantly male, despite women forming a slight majority in the sector overall. Additionally, income progression is closely linked to seniority, control, and scale rather than effort alone. Self-employment is also highlighted as a pathway offering greater financial potential but increased volatility. While agents remain ambitious and engaged, many express concerns about their long-term professional direction.

The Prime Cut: Understanding the Upper Market

Accompanying the People & Salary report is The Prime Cut, which provides a detailed analysis of the UK’s upper quartile, prime, and super-prime residential markets. The report challenges the notion of the prime market as a single entity, instead describing it as "three completely different industries operating under the same job title." This segmentation underscores the complexities and nuances of catering to high-net-worth individuals and underscores the need for specialised expertise in this sector.

The Faculty: Bridging Insight and Action

In tandem with these reports, The Faculty has been introduced as a practical training platform designed to turn industry data into actionable strategies. Simon Leadbetter, founder of We Are Unchained and Curator of The Voice of the Agent, emphasised the initiative’s broader ambition: "Estate agency has never lacked opinion. What it has often lacked is structured evidence, joined-up thinking, and the capability to consistently act on what the industry already knows." The Faculty aims to address this gap by equipping professionals with the tools to navigate operational challenges and drive development across the sector.

As The Voice of the Agent continues to evolve, its focus on research, insight, and practical training highlights its commitment to supporting estate agency professionals in an increasingly complex and competitive market.

item.title: Rental arrears hit record high as tenant pressure continues
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Rental arrears in the UK have reached a record high, according to recent data, as tenants continue to grapple with financial pressures. The first quarter of 2026 saw average arrears climb to £2,281, marking a 2% year-on-year increase. Whilst this rise is notably smaller compared to previous years, it underscores the ongoing challenges faced by renters amidst elevated living costs and borrowing rates.

Trends in Rental Arrears

The latest figures suggest a potential stabilisation in the rental market, albeit at higher levels of arrears. Between Q1 2023 and Q1 2024, arrears surged by 27%, followed by a further 23% increase the following year. However, the recent slowdown in growth indicates that conditions may be beginning to ease. This trend is mirrored in the buy-to-let sector, where mortgage arrears fell quarter-on-quarter by the end of Q4 2025, according to UK Finance.

Despite these signs of stabilisation, affordability remains a significant concern. Inflation and relatively high interest rates continue to strain household budgets, impacting both tenants and landlords. Ben Grech, CEO of Reposit, highlighted the changing dynamics in the rental market, noting that landlords are becoming increasingly cautious. “With the Renters’ Rights Act now in place and the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions, landlords understandably have reduced flexibility in managing tenancies,” he explained.

Landlord Caution and Tenant Challenges

The shift in landlord behaviour is partly driven by legislative changes and the financial risks associated with renting. Traditional deposit schemes, which average £1,308, fall short of the current average arrears value of £2,281. This discrepancy highlights the limitations of such schemes in providing adequate protection for landlords. Grech emphasised the need for solutions that reduce risk and improve affordability, stating, “Solutions that offer greater protection for landlords while allowing tenants to retain access to their money will be crucial in supporting a balanced and resilient rental market.”

For tenants, the financial strain extends beyond rent payments. Many are also dealing with rising moving costs and other expenses, making it difficult to set aside funds for deposits. Grech suggested that alternative schemes could help renters by allowing them to use their money for immediate needs or invest it for returns, rather than locking it away for several years.

As the rental market continues to evolve, both tenants and landlords face ongoing challenges. Whilst the pace of arrears growth has slowed, affordability pressures remain a key issue. With legislative changes reshaping the landscape, finding innovative solutions to support all parties will be essential in fostering a more stable rental sector.

item.title: Is Matthew Pennycook signalling a national rollout of the London housing model?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

London’s housing crisis has reached a critical juncture, with new home registrations plummeting by 59% year-on-year in early 2025. Despite ambitious targets under the New London Plan, only 904 homes were registered in the capital during Q2 – a stark shortfall against the 22,000 quarterly homes needed. This alarming trend has prompted emergency government interventions, raising questions about whether similar measures might soon apply nationwide.

London’s emergency housing measures

The government and Mayor of London recently unveiled the Homes for London policy paper, introducing temporary measures to stimulate construction. Key proposals include:

  • Reducing affordable housing requirements from 35% to 20% for qualifying schemes
  • Providing Community Infrastructure Levy (CIL) relief for brownfield developments
  • Relaxing specific London Plan policies to improve project viability

These changes aim to address multiple barriers to development, including soaring build costs, tighter financing conditions and increasingly complex regulations. Industry experts suggest the capital’s challenges mirror those facing housing delivery across England, where many local authorities are also struggling to meet housing targets.

Could national policy follow London’s lead?

Shadow Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook recently hinted at potential flexibility in affordable housing requirements for new towns, describing the 40% target as “an aspiration” rather than a fixed rule. This statement has sparked speculation that ministers might adopt London-style viability assessments more widely.

The London measures recognise several systemic issues affecting housing delivery:

  • Rigid application of planning policies reducing scheme viability
  • Increasing construction and financing costs squeezing margins
  • Growing regulatory burdens delaying projects

With the government’s 12 proposed new towns facing similar viability challenges, policymakers may need to consider whether temporary relaxations of planning requirements could help accelerate housing delivery beyond the capital.

As the housing shortage continues across England, the London experiment could become a testing ground for national policy reforms. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor how these emergency measures affect both housing supply and affordability in the coming months.

item.title: Homebuyers remain sceptical about AI replacing estate agents
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Despite advancements in AI technology, most UK homebuyers and sellers still favour human interaction over artificial intelligence during crucial stages of the property process. According to recent research by Moneypenny, which surveyed 2,000 adults, 83% of respondents would prefer to deal with a real estate agent when booking valuations or making offers. Just 6% expressed a preference for using AI in these scenarios, suggesting a continued reliance on human expertise for high-stakes decisions.

AI Acceptance Varies Across Property Tasks

The study reveals that comfort levels with AI depend largely on the complexity of the task at hand. Whilst 31% of respondents said they would be comfortable using AI for general property queries, this figure drops significantly for more intricate processes. Only 28% would consider booking a viewing via AI, and just 18% would use it to arrange a valuation. When it comes to transactional updates, such as chasing progress on a sale or purchase, comfort levels fall further, with 17% and 16% respectively expressing willingness. Notably, only 8% said they would be happy to use AI for making or accepting an offer, highlighting a preference for human involvement in key negotiations.

Interestingly, 40% of respondents stated they would not feel comfortable using AI at any stage of the property process. This reluctance underscores the importance of personalised, human-led services in the property market, particularly for decisions involving significant financial commitments.

Generational and Regional Differences

The research also highlights notable differences in attitudes across age groups and regions. Baby Boomers are the most resistant to AI, with 90% preferring human interaction and 53% stating they would not use AI at all during the property process. In contrast, only 16% of Gen Z respondents shared this view, and 23% said they would be comfortable using AI to make or accept an offer. Millennials and Gen X respondents fell somewhere in between, with 75% of Millennials preferring human interaction and just 7% of Gen X willing to use AI for offers.

Regional variations were also apparent. Scotland, Northern Ireland, and the East of England showed the strongest preference for human interaction, with 87% of respondents in these areas favouring it. Meanwhile, London, Yorkshire and Humberside, and the South East were more open to AI, with 11% of respondents in each region saying they would use it to make or accept an offer. Scotland also had the highest proportion of respondents (48%) who would not use AI at all in the property process.

Gender differences emerged too, with 47% of women stating they would not feel comfortable using AI at any stage, compared to a third of men. Men were more likely to accept AI for transactional updates, such as chasing progress on a purchase.

These findings suggest that whilst AI may have a role in simpler aspects of the property journey, such as answering general queries, its acceptance dwindles when it comes to high-value or high-stakes decisions. Homebuyers and sellers continue to place their trust in the expertise and reassurance provided by human estate agents, particularly during critical moments of the transaction process.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Despite advancements in AI technology, most UK homebuyers and sellers still favour human interaction over artificial intelligence during crucial stages of the property process. According to recent research by Moneypenny, which surveyed 2,000 adults, 83% of respondents would prefer to deal with a real estate agent when booking valuations or making offers. Just 6% expressed a preference for using AI in these scenarios, suggesting a continued reliance on human expertise for high-stakes decisions.

AI Acceptance Varies Across Property Tasks

The study reveals that comfort levels with AI depend largely on the complexity of the task at hand. Whilst 31% of respondents said they would be comfortable using AI for general property queries, this figure drops significantly for more intricate processes. Only 28% would consider booking a viewing via AI, and just 18% would use it to arrange a valuation. When it comes to transactional updates, such as chasing progress on a sale or purchase, comfort levels fall further, with 17% and 16% respectively expressing willingness. Notably, only 8% said they would be happy to use AI for making or accepting an offer, highlighting a preference for human involvement in key negotiations.

Interestingly, 40% of respondents stated they would not feel comfortable using AI at any stage of the property process. This reluctance underscores the importance of personalised, human-led services in the property market, particularly for decisions involving significant financial commitments.

Generational and Regional Differences

The research also highlights notable differences in attitudes across age groups and regions. Baby Boomers are the most resistant to AI, with 90% preferring human interaction and 53% stating they would not use AI at all during the property process. In contrast, only 16% of Gen Z respondents shared this view, and 23% said they would be comfortable using AI to make or accept an offer. Millennials and Gen X respondents fell somewhere in between, with 75% of Millennials preferring human interaction and just 7% of Gen X willing to use AI for offers.

Regional variations were also apparent. Scotland, Northern Ireland, and the East of England showed the strongest preference for human interaction, with 87% of respondents in these areas favouring it. Meanwhile, London, Yorkshire and Humberside, and the South East were more open to AI, with 11% of respondents in each region saying they would use it to make or accept an offer. Scotland also had the highest proportion of respondents (48%) who would not use AI at all in the property process.

Gender differences emerged too, with 47% of women stating they would not feel comfortable using AI at any stage, compared to a third of men. Men were more likely to accept AI for transactional updates, such as chasing progress on a purchase.

These findings suggest that whilst AI may have a role in simpler aspects of the property journey, such as answering general queries, its acceptance dwindles when it comes to high-value or high-stakes decisions. Homebuyers and sellers continue to place their trust in the expertise and reassurance provided by human estate agents, particularly during critical moments of the transaction process.

item.title: LonRes rolls out AI tool to extend property visibility beyond its network
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

LonRes has introduced an innovative AI-powered tool designed to enhance property visibility for its subscribers. The new feature, named AI Boost, allows estate agents to extend the reach of their listings beyond the LonRes network to popular AI search platforms at no additional cost.

How AI Boost Works

The service operates through a simple one-click activation within the LonRes system. Once enabled, selected property listings become accessible to AI-driven search platforms via API integration. Importantly, the tool maintains existing safeguards:

  • Off-market properties remain excluded
  • Restricted listings won't appear in searches
  • Full addresses aren't displayed to maintain privacy

When potential buyers discover properties through AI platforms like ChatGPT or Perplexity, they're directed to a dedicated property page containing key details while ensuring all enquiries route back to the original listing agent.

A New Marketing Channel for Agents

LonRes positions AI Boost as a complementary tool rather than a replacement for traditional property portals. Chris Welch, LonRes Sales Director, explained: "This technology represents an additional marketing channel that could help subscribers reach buyers who are increasingly using AI platforms for property searches."

The service offers agents complete control over which properties they promote, with all selections managed within LonRes's secure platform environment. As AI property search technology develops, LonRes suggests this initiative might help members stay ahead in an evolving digital landscape.

For estate agents using LonRes, AI Boost might present an opportunity to test new methods of property promotion without additional costs or compromising existing marketing strategies. The tool's integration maintains current privacy controls while potentially expanding audience reach through emerging search technologies.

item.title: Solicitor-Reviewed: A Clause-by-Clause Guide to Tenancy Agreements Under the Renters’ Rights Act
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Understanding the Updated Tenancy Agreement Under the Renters’ Rights Act

With the Renters’ Rights Act coming into force on 1st May 2026, landlords and letting agencies are revising their tenancy agreements to comply with the new legislation. Lendlord, a prominent player in the rental market, has taken the proactive step of completely rewriting its standard tenancy contract. The company’s CEO, Aviram Shahar, recently collaborated with Emma Damiani, a solicitor from Ackroyd Legal, to ensure the document meets all legal requirements.

Ackroyd Legal, an SRA-regulated London law firm, specialises in advising landlords and property investors on complex legal matters. Their involvement highlights the importance of ensuring tenancy agreements are both legally sound and fair to all parties. Homeowners and landlords may wish to review their own contracts to avoid potential disputes or non-compliance issues once the Act takes effect.

Key Considerations for Landlords and Tenants

The Renters’ Rights Act introduces several changes designed to strengthen tenant protections whilst maintaining landlords’ rights. Some of the key areas affected include:

  • Notice periods for evictions
  • Rules surrounding rent increases
  • Tenants’ rights to request property improvements
  • Clauses relating to deposit protection

Borrowers with buy-to-let mortgages might consider reviewing their tenancy agreements well in advance of the May 2026 deadline. Seeking professional legal advice could help ensure documents remain enforceable whilst protecting both landlord and tenant interests. Property investors may find it worthwhile to consult specialists like Ackroyd Legal for tailored guidance on their specific circumstances.

As the rental market adapts to these legislative changes, staying informed about tenancy agreement updates could prove valuable for all parties involved in the lettings process.

item.title: Labour government faces growing housing challenge as public confidence slips
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Public confidence in the government’s housing policies appears to be waning, according to recent polling data. A survey by Ipsos reveals just 17% of respondents believe the current administration is handling housing issues effectively – a four-point drop since May 2025. The findings highlight ongoing challenges around affordability, supply shortages and rental market pressures across the UK.

Housing pledges meet market realities

Labour came to power with ambitious housing commitments, including plans to build 1.5 million homes during this parliamentary term and reforms to streamline planning processes. The government has also progressed rental sector changes, notably proposals to end Section 21 evictions. However, market conditions remain challenging, with sluggish housebuilding rates, constrained planning approvals and mortgage affordability continuing to hinder first-time buyers despite recent interest rate stabilisation.

The polling suggests voters remain divided on which party offers the strongest housing strategy. While 25% favoured Reform UK’s approach, 23% preferred the Green Party’s policies and 22% backed the Conservatives. This fragmented response indicates widespread dissatisfaction with housing delivery across multiple administrations, particularly regarding homeownership accessibility and rental costs.

Policy implementation under scrutiny

Industry observers suggest the government faces mounting pressure to demonstrate tangible progress in several key areas:

  • Accelerating housing delivery through planning system reforms
  • Improving affordability metrics for prospective buyers
  • Ensuring rental reforms achieve meaningful tenant protections
  • Stimulating transaction activity across the property market

Ben Marshall of Ipsos UK noted the findings reflect growing openness among voters to consider alternative policy approaches. The data emerges as ministers work to translate election pledges into measurable outcomes, with housing remaining a critical issue for many households.

As the government approaches its second year in office, property market stakeholders will be watching closely for evidence that core housing commitments are translating into improved conditions for buyers, renters and developers alike.

item.title: Lloyds set to launch £5,000 deposit mortgage for first-time buyers
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Lloyds Banking Group has announced the launch of a new mortgage product tailored for first-time buyers struggling to save for a large deposit. Set to become available from 18 May, the deal allows borrowers to secure a mortgage with a deposit as low as £5,000, provided it comes from their own savings rather than being gifted. Offered at a fixed rate of 5.89% over five years, the product is designed for properties valued up to £300,000. This initiative aims to help renters facing high housing costs, particularly those without family financial support, take their first step onto the property ladder.

Key Features of the Lloyds Mortgage

The new mortgage product, available through Lloyds, Halifax and intermediary channels, offers several notable features. Borrowers can access loans of up to 4.5 times their income, subject to affordability and credit checks. The maximum loan-to-value ratio is just over 98%, meaning buyers need only a minimal deposit to secure a property. The mortgage term can extend up to 40 years, providing flexibility for those seeking lower monthly repayments. Additionally, the product comes with no arrangement fee, reducing upfront costs for borrowers.

However, there are some limitations to be aware of. The mortgage is not available for shared ownership purchases, new-build properties, or applications involving gifted deposits. Lloyds has emphasised that the product is particularly aimed at renters who are already paying high monthly housing costs, often comparable to mortgage repayments. By lowering the upfront deposit requirement, the bank hopes to reduce the time needed for buyers to save and make home ownership more accessible.

A Solution to the Deposit Dilemma

Amanda Bryden, head of mortgages at Lloyds, highlighted the challenges faced by many first-time buyers. “Many people are managing their finances well but still feel locked out of home ownership because saving a large deposit seems impossible,” she said. “This product aims to break down that barrier by reducing the upfront cost to £5,000, giving more people the opportunity to own their first home and build a secure future.”

Industry experts have welcomed the move. David Hollingworth, associate director at L&C Mortgages, noted the significance of the launch, stating, “First-time buyers often struggle to save even a 5% deposit, especially when high rents make saving difficult. This product offers a valuable solution for those who don’t have access to family financial support.”

With the gap between average rents and mortgage repayments narrowing in recent years, this new mortgage product could prove a game-changer for many aspiring homeowners. By addressing the deposit hurdle, Lloyds is offering a practical route onto the property ladder for those who might otherwise feel excluded.
